subject: Physics
q: A photograph of a landscape is captured by a drone camera at a height of 18 km . The size of the camera film is $2 \mathrm{~cm} \times 2 \mathrm{~cm}$ and the area of the landscape photographed is $400 \mathrm{~km}^2$. The focal length of the lens in the drone camera is :
question: A photograph of a landscape is captured by a drone camera at a height of 18 km . The size of the camera film is $2 \mathrm{~cm} \times 2 \mathrm{~cm}$ and the area of the landscape photographed is $400 \mathrm{~km}^2$. The focal length of the lens in the drone camera is :
solution: $\mathrm{H}=18 \mathrm{~km}$ Size of camera film $=2 \mathrm{~cm} \times 2 \mathrm{~cm}$ $\begin{aligned} & A_{\text {image }}=400 \mathrm{~km}^2 \\ & x=20 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~m}=2 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~m} \\ & y=2 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m} \\ & \frac{x}{y}=10^6=\frac{18 \mathrm{Km}}{\mathrm{f}} \\ & \mathrm{f}=18 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}=18 \mathrm{~mm} \\ & \mathrm{f}=1.8 \mathrm{~cm} \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Given a thin convex lens (refractive index $\mu_2$ ), kept in a liquid (refractive index $\mu_1, \mu_1 \lt \mu_2$ ) having radii of curvatures $\left|R_1\right|$ and $\left|R_2\right|$. Its second surface is silver polished. Where should an object be placed on the optic axis so that a real and inverted image is formed at the same place?
question: Given a thin convex lens (refractive index $\mu_2$ ), kept in a liquid (refractive index $\mu_1, \mu_1 \lt \mu_2$ ) having radii of curvatures $\left|R_1\right|$ and $\left|R_2\right|$. Its second surface is silver polished. Where should an object be placed on the optic axis so that a real and inverted image is formed at the same place?
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{eq}}}=\frac{2}{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{L}}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{m}}} \\ & \mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{m}}=-\frac{\left|\mathrm{R}_2\right|}{2} \\ & \frac{1}{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{L}}}=\left(\frac{\mu_2}{\mu_1}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_1}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_2}\right)\end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{eq}}}=2\left(\frac{\mu_2-\mu_1}{\mu_1}\right)\left(\frac{\mathrm{R}_1+\mathrm{R}_2}{\mathrm{R}_1 \mathrm{R}_2}\right)+\frac{2}{\mathrm{R}_2} \\ & =\frac{2}{\mathrm{R}_2}\left[\frac{\left(\mu_2-\mu_1\right)\left(\mathrm{R}_1+\mathrm{R}_2\right)+\mu_1 \mathrm{R}_1}{\mu_1 \mathrm{R}_1}\right] \\ & =\frac{2}{\mathrm{R}_2}\left[\frac{\mu_2 \mathrm{R}_1+\mu_2 \mathrm{R}_2-\mu_1 \mathrm{R}_1-\mu_1 \mathrm{R}_2+\mu_1 \mathrm{R}_1}{\mu_1 \mathrm{R}_1}\right] \\ & \frac{1}{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{eq}}}=\frac{2\left[\mu_2 \mathrm{R}_1+\mu_2 \mathrm{R}_2-\mu_1 \mathrm{R}_2\right]}{\mu_1 \mathrm{R}_1 \mathrm{R}_2} \end{aligned}$ For same size of image $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{u}=2 \mathrm{f} \\ & \mathrm{u}=\frac{\mu_1 \mathrm{R}_1 \mathrm{R}_2}{\mu_2 \mathrm{R}_1+\mu_2 \mathrm{R}_2-\mu_1 \mathrm{R}_2} \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Two transparent media having refractive indices <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> are separated by a spherical refracting surface of radius of curvature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The centre of curvature of surface is towards denser medium and a point object is placed on the principal axis in rarer medium at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from the pole of the surface. The distance of image from the pole of the surface is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math>.
question: Two transparent media having refractive indices <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> are separated by a spherical refracting surface of radius of curvature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The centre of curvature of surface is towards denser medium and a point object is placed on the principal axis in rarer medium at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from the pole of the surface. The distance of image from the pole of the surface is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math>.
solution:
Let's consider the following diagram-

The relation between the object distance, the image distance and the radius of curvature of the spherical surface can be written as

μ2v-μ1u=μ2-μ1R   ...1

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (1) and solve to calculate the required image distance.

1.5v-1-15=1.5-1.030⇒1.5v=160-115=-120⇒v=-30

Hence, the image is formed at a distance of 30 cm from the pole on the same direction as the object is placed.

subject: Physics
q: A light ray enters a solid glass sphere of refractive index μ=3 at an angle of incidence 60°. The ray is both reflected and refracted at the farther surface of the sphere. The angle (in degrees) between the reflected and refracted rays at this surface is _____________.
question: A light ray enters a solid glass sphere of refractive index μ=3 at an angle of incidence 60°. The ray is both reflected and refracted at the farther surface of the sphere. The angle (in degrees) between the reflected and refracted rays at this surface is _____________.
solution:
Apply Snell's law at S_
1sin 60°=3sin r
sin r=12
r=30°
from geometry
r'=30°
Again apply snell's law on S2
3sinr'=1sine
32=sine
∴e=60°
from geometry
r'+θ+e=180°
θ=90°

subject: Physics
q: For the thin convex lens, the radii of curvature are at $15 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $30 \mathrm{~cm}$ respectively. The focal length the lens is $20 \mathrm{~cm}$. The refractive index of the material is :
question: For the thin convex lens, the radii of curvature are at $15 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $30 \mathrm{~cm}$ respectively. The focal length the lens is $20 \mathrm{~cm}$. The refractive index of the material is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{\mathrm{f}}=\left(\frac{\mu_{\text {lens }}}{\mu_{\text {air }}}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_1}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_2}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{+20}=\left(\frac{\mu}{1}-1\right)\left(\frac{1}{+15}-\frac{1}{(-30)}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{20}=(\mu-1)\left(\frac{3}{30}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \mu-1=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow \mu=1+\frac{1}{2}=\frac{3}{2}=1 \cdot 5\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
A lens having refractive index 1.6 has focal length of 12 cm , when it is in air. Find the focal length of the lens when it is placed in water.
(Take refractive index of water as 1.28)
question:
A lens having refractive index 1.6 has focal length of 12 cm , when it is in air. Find the focal length of the lens when it is placed in water.
(Take refractive index of water as 1.28)
solution:
As we know,
$\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}}=\left[\frac{\mu_{\mathrm{L}}}{\mu_{\mathrm{m}}}-1\right]\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_1}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_2}\right]$
For air $\mu_{\mathrm{m}}=1$
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{12}=[1.6-1]\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_1}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_2}\right] \\ & \frac{1}{12}=\frac{6}{10}\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_1}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_2}\right] \\ & {\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_1}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}_2}\right]=\frac{10}{72}}\end{aligned}$
For water
$\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}}=\left[\frac{1.6}{1.28}-1\right]\left[\frac{10}{72}\right]=\frac{32}{128} \times \frac{10}{72}$
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{\mathrm{f}}=\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{10}{72} \\ & \mathrm{f}=28.8 \mathrm{~cm} \\ & \mathrm{f}=288 \mathrm{~mm}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A transparent film of refractive index, 2.0 is coated on a glass slab of refractive index, 1.45. What is the minimum thickness of transparent film to be coated for the maximum transmission of Green light of wavelength 550 nm . [Assume that the light is incident nearly perpendicular to the glass surface.]
question: A transparent film of refractive index, 2.0 is coated on a glass slab of refractive index, 1.45. What is the minimum thickness of transparent film to be coated for the maximum transmission of Green light of wavelength 550 nm . [Assume that the light is incident nearly perpendicular to the glass surface.]
solution: For transmitted green light to be maxima, reflected green should be minima. $\begin{aligned} & \Delta \mathrm{P}=2 \mu_0 \mathrm{t}=\mathrm{n} \lambda \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=\frac{\mathrm{n} \lambda}{2 \mu_0} \therefore \mathrm{t}_{\min }=\frac{\lambda}{2 \mu_0}=\frac{550}{2 \times 2}=137.5 \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A light ray is incident perpendicular to one face of a $90^{\circ}$ prism and is totally internally reflected at the glass-air interface. If the angle of reflection is $45^{\circ}$, we conclude that the refractive index $n$
question: A light ray is incident perpendicular to one face of a $90^{\circ}$ prism and is totally internally reflected at the glass-air interface. If the angle of reflection is $45^{\circ}$, we conclude that the refractive index $n$
solution:
Angle of incidence $\mathrm{i}>\mathrm{C}$ for total internal reflection. Here $\mathrm{i}=45^{\circ}$ inside the medium.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \therefore 45^{\circ}>\sin ^{-1}(1 / n) \\
& \Rightarrow n>\sqrt{ } 2 .
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q: Refer the figure given below. $\mu_1$ and $\mu_2$ are refractive indices of air and lens material. The height of image will be _______ cm.
question: Refer the figure given below. $\mu_1$ and $\mu_2$ are refractive indices of air and lens material. The height of image will be _______ cm.
solution:
Taking the pole $P$ as the origin and the direction of incident light as positive:
$\mu_1 = 1$ (air), $\mu_2 = 1.5$ (lens)
$u = -40\text{ cm}$
$R = -20\text{ cm}$ (centre of curvature lies to the left of $P$)
$h_o = +2\text{ cm}$

Using the formula for refraction at a single spherical surface:
$\dfrac{\mu_2}{v} - \dfrac{\mu_1}{u} = \dfrac{\mu_2 - \mu_1}{R}$

$\dfrac{1.5}{v} - \dfrac{1}{-40} = \dfrac{1.5 - 1}{-20}$

$\dfrac{1.5}{v} + \dfrac{1}{40} = -\dfrac{1}{40}$

$\dfrac{1.5}{v} = -\dfrac{2}{40} = -\dfrac{1}{20}$

$v = -30\text{ cm}$

The negative sign indicates a virtual image formed $30\text{ cm}$ to the left of $P$.

The transverse magnification for refraction at a spherical surface is:
$m = \dfrac{\mu_1 \cdot v}{\mu_2 \cdot u} = \dfrac{1 \cdot (-30)}{1.5 \cdot (-40)} = \dfrac{-30}{-60} = 0.5$

Therefore:
$h_i = m \cdot h_o = 0.5 \times 2 = 1\text{ cm}$

Hence, the correct option is $(1)\ 1$.

subject: Physics
q: In an experiment for determination of refractive index of glass of a prism by <math><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></math> v/s <math><mi mathvariant="normal">δ</mi></math> plot, it was found that a ray incident at angle <math><msup><mrow><mn>35</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup></math> , suffers a deviation of <math><msup><mrow><mn>40</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup></math> and that it emerges at angle <math><msup><mrow><mn>79</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup></math> . In that case which of the following is closest to the maximum possible value of the refractive index?
question: In an experiment for determination of refractive index of glass of a prism by <math><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi></math> v/s <math><mi mathvariant="normal">δ</mi></math> plot, it was found that a ray incident at angle <math><msup><mrow><mn>35</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup></math> , suffers a deviation of <math><msup><mrow><mn>40</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup></math> and that it emerges at angle <math><msup><mrow><mn>79</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup></math> . In that case which of the following is closest to the maximum possible value of the refractive index?
solution:
i=35o, δ=40o, e=79o

δ=i+e-A

40o=35o+79o-A

A=74o

And r1+r2=A=74o

Since δmin<40o

μ<sin⁡74+402sin⁡37

μmax=1.44

solution:
As $\lambda_{\text {red }}>\lambda_{\text {yellow }}>\lambda_{\text {violet }}$
Light ray with longer wavelength bends less.

q: A point source of light, S is placed at a distance L in front of the center of plane mirror of width d which is hanging vertically on a wall. A man walks in front of the mirror along a line parallel to the mirror, at a distance 2L as shown below. The distance over which the man can see the image of the light source in the mirror is:
question: A point source of light, S is placed at a distance L in front of the center of plane mirror of width d which is hanging vertically on a wall. A man walks in front of the mirror along a line parallel to the mirror, at a distance 2L as shown below. The distance over which the man can see the image of the light source in the mirror is:
solution:
By similar triangles

ΔAEC~ΔIHA

y2L=d2L (by properties of similar triangles)
⇒y=d
∴Total distance CD=y+d2+d2+y=3d

subject: Physics
q: The difference of speed of light in the two media <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>A</mi></msub><mo>-</mo><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>7</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If the refractive index of medium <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>47</mn></math>, then the ratio of refractive index of medium <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> to medium <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is: (Given : speed of light in vacuum <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: The difference of speed of light in the two media <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>A</mi></msub><mo>-</mo><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>B</mi></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>7</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. If the refractive index of medium <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>47</mn></math>, then the ratio of refractive index of medium <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> to medium <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is: (Given : speed of light in vacuum <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>8</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
The speed of light in the medium B will be, vB=cμB

The speed of light in the medium A will be, vA=cμA.

Now, 

vA-vB=c1μA-1μB⇒2.6×107=3×108×1μA-11.47⇒μA=1.13

question: The position of the image formed by the combination of lenses is :
solution:
For lens $1: \mathrm{f}_1=10, \mathrm{u}=-30, \mathrm{v}=$ ? $v=\frac{u f}{u+f}=\frac{-30 \times 10}{-30+10}=15$ For lens 2: $\mathrm{f}_1=-10, \mathrm{u}=10, \mathrm{v}=$ ? $v=\frac{u f}{u+f}=\frac{10 \times-10}{10-10}=\infty$
For lens $3: \mathrm{f}=30, \mathrm{u}=-\infty, \mathrm{v}=$ ? So v will be 30 .

subject: Physics
q: Light from a point source in air falls on a spherical glass surface (refractive index, $\mu=1.5$ and radius of curvature $=50 \mathrm{~cm}$). The image is formed at a distance of 200 cm from the glass surface inside the glass. The magnitude of distance of the light source from the glass surface is ____ m.
question: Light from a point source in air falls on a spherical glass surface (refractive index, $\mu=1.5$ and radius of curvature $=50 \mathrm{~cm}$). The image is formed at a distance of 200 cm from the glass surface inside the glass. The magnitude of distance of the light source from the glass surface is ____ m.
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mu_2}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{\mu_1}{\mathrm{u}}=\frac{\mu_2-\mu_1}{\mathrm{R}} \\ & \frac{1.5}{200}-\frac{1}{-\mathrm{x}}=\frac{1.5-1}{50}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{x}=\frac{1}{100}-\frac{3}{400} \\ & x=400 \mathrm{~cm} \\ & x=4 m\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A convergent doublet of separated lenses, corrected for spherical aberration, has resultant focal length of $10 \mathrm{~cm}$. The separation between the two lenses is $2 \mathrm{~cm}$. The focal lengths of the component lenses
question: A convergent doublet of separated lenses, corrected for spherical aberration, has resultant focal length of $10 \mathrm{~cm}$. The separation between the two lenses is $2 \mathrm{~cm}$. The focal lengths of the component lenses
solution:
For minimum spherical aberration separation, $d=f_1-f_2=2 \mathrm{~cm}$
Resultant focal length $=F=10 \mathrm{~cm}$
Using $\frac{1}{F}=\frac{1}{f_1}+\frac{1}{f_2}-\frac{d}{f_1 f_2}$ and solving, we get $\mathrm{f}_1, \mathrm{f}_2 18 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $20 \mathrm{~cm}$ respectively.

subject: Physics
q: In parallax method for the determination of focal length of a concave mirror, the object should always be placed:
question: In parallax method for the determination of focal length of a concave mirror, the object should always be placed:
options: between the focus $(F)$ and the centre of curvature $(C)$ of the mirror ONLY
between the pole($P$) and the focus $(F)$ of the concave mirror ONLY
beyond the centre of the curvature $(C)$ of the mirror ONLY
at any point beyond the focus $(F)$ of the mirror
solution:
In the parallax method for determining the focal length of a concave mirror, we use an object pin and its real image formed by the mirror.
The method of 'no parallax' involves aligning the tip of the object pin with the tip of its real image. This is only possible if the image formed is real.
For a concave mirror, a real image is formed only when the object is placed at any position beyond the focus ($F$), i.e., $u > f$.
If the object is placed between the pole ($P$) and the focus ($F$), the image formed is virtual, and it cannot be caught on a screen or used for the standard parallax alignment with a physical pin in front of the mirror.
Therefore, the object should be placed at any point beyond the focus ($F$) of the mirror to ensure a real image is formed.

subject: Physics
q: As shown in the figure, a plane mirror is fixed at a height of 50 cm from the bottom of tank containing water μ=43. The height of water in the tank is 8 cm. A small bulb is placed at the bottom of the water tank. The distance of image of the bulb formed by mirror from the bottom of the tank is  ______ cm.
question: As shown in the figure, a plane mirror is fixed at a height of 50 cm from the bottom of tank containing water μ=43. The height of water in the tank is 8 cm. A small bulb is placed at the bottom of the water tank. The distance of image of the bulb formed by mirror from the bottom of the tank is  ______ cm.
solution:
The data given is 

d=8 cmμ=43

Apparent depth is

 h'=dμ=843=6 cm
Distance of object from mirror =42+6=48 m
Distance of image from bottom of tank =48+50=98 m

subject: Physics
q: A concave mirror for face viewing has a focal length of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0.4</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The distance at which you hold the mirror from your face in order to see your image upright with a magnification of <math><mn>5</mn></math> is
question: A concave mirror for face viewing has a focal length of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0.4</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>. The distance at which you hold the mirror from your face in order to see your image upright with a magnification of <math><mn>5</mn></math> is
solution:
m=5=-vu        ⇒v=-5u
f=-0.4 m
From mirror formula,
1v+1u=1f
1-5u+1u=1-0.4
-1+55u=1-0.4
u=45-0.4=-0.32 m
So, distance of object from mirror=0.32 m

subject: Physics
q: An object is placed beyond the centre of curvature C of the given concave mirror. If the distance of the object is d1 from C and the distance of the image formed is d2 from C, the radius of curvature of this mirror is:
question: An object is placed beyond the centre of curvature C of the given concave mirror. If the distance of the object is d1 from C and the distance of the image formed is d2 from C, the radius of curvature of this mirror is:
solution:
xy=f2

f+d1f-d2=f2

f=d1d2d1-d2

R=2f=2d1d2d1-d2

subject: Physics
q: If two mirrors are kept at $60^{\circ}$ to each other, then the number of images formed by them is
question: If two mirrors are kept at $60^{\circ}$ to each other, then the number of images formed by them is
solution: No. of images, $n=(360 / \theta)-1$. As $\theta=60^{\circ}$ so $n=5$

subject: Physics
q: Curved surfaces of a plano-convex lens of refractive index μ1 and a plano-concave lens of refractive index μ2 have equal radius of curvature as shown in figure. Find the ratio of radius of curvature to the focal length of the combined lenses
question: Curved surfaces of a plano-convex lens of refractive index μ1 and a plano-concave lens of refractive index μ2 have equal radius of curvature as shown in figure. Find the ratio of radius of curvature to the focal length of the combined lenses
solution:
1f1=μ1-11R

1f2=μ2-11-R⇒1feq=1f1+1f2=μ1-1R-μ2-1R

1feq=μ1-μ2R⇒Rfeq=μ1-μ2

subject: Physics
q: One plano-convex and one plano-concave lens of the same radius of curvature R but of different materials are joined side by side as shown in the figure. If the refractive index of the material of 1 is μ1 and that of 2 is μ2, then the focal length of the combination is:
question: One plano-convex and one plano-concave lens of the same radius of curvature R but of different materials are joined side by side as shown in the figure. If the refractive index of the material of 1 is μ1 and that of 2 is μ2, then the focal length of the combination is:
solution:
From lens maker’s formula,
1f=μ-11R1-1R2
1f1=μ1-11∞-1-R=μ1-1R
1f2=μ2-11-R-1∞=-μ2-1R
1feq=1f1+1f2=μ1-1R-μ2-1R
=μ1-μ2R
feq=Rμ1-μ2

question: Two identical symmetric double convex lenses of focal length $f$ are cut into two equal parts $L_1, L_2$ by $A B$ plane and $L_3, L_4$ by $X Y$ plane as shown in figure respectively. The ratio of focal lengths of lenses $L_1$ and $L_3$ is
solution: so $\frac{f_1}{f_3}=1: 2$

subject: Physics
q: A spherical surface of radius of curvature $R$, separates air from glass (refractive index $=1.5$ ). The centre of curvature is in the glass medium. A point object ' $O$ ' placed in air on the optic axis of the surface, so that its real image is formed at ' $I$ ' inside glass. The line OI intersects the spherical surface at P and $\mathrm{PO}=\mathrm{PI}$. The distance PO equals to
question: A spherical surface of radius of curvature $R$, separates air from glass (refractive index $=1.5$ ). The centre of curvature is in the glass medium. A point object ' $O$ ' placed in air on the optic axis of the surface, so that its real image is formed at ' $I$ ' inside glass. The line OI intersects the spherical surface at P and $\mathrm{PO}=\mathrm{PI}$. The distance PO equals to
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{PO}=\mathrm{u}=-\mathrm{x} \\ & \mathrm{PI}=\mathrm{v}=\mathrm{x} \\ & \mathrm{PO}=\mathrm{PI} \\ & \frac{\mu_2}{\mathrm{v}}-\frac{\mu_1}{\mathrm{u}}=\frac{\mu_2-\mu_1}{\mathrm{R}} \\ & \frac{1.5}{\mathrm{x}}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{x}}=\frac{1}{2 \mathrm{R}} \\ & \frac{5}{2 \mathrm{x}}=\frac{1}{2 \mathrm{R}} \\ & \mathrm{X}=5 \mathrm{R}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
In figure, the optical fiber is l=2 m long and has a diameter of d=20 μm. If a ray of light is incident on one end of the fiber at angle θ1=40° , the number of reflections it makes before emerging from the other end is close to:
(refractive index of fiber is 1.31 , sin 40°=0.64 and sin-1⁡0.49=30° .)
question:
In figure, the optical fiber is l=2 m long and has a diameter of d=20 μm. If a ray of light is incident on one end of the fiber at angle θ1=40° , the number of reflections it makes before emerging from the other end is close to:
(refractive index of fiber is 1.31 , sin 40°=0.64 and sin-1⁡0.49=30° .)
solution:
Let n1 and n2 be the refractive indices of the air and optical fibre respectively. Applying Snell's law at Q, we get:

n1sinθ1=n2sinθ2

sin40∘=1.31×sinθ2

⇒sinθ2=0.641.31=0.4885≈0.5

⇒θ2=30∘

The distance covered along the length of the optical fibre after one reflection:

QN=RN cotθ2

=20×10-6×cot30∘=203×10-6 m

The reflections take place throughout the length of the optical fibre.

Length of the wire=2 m

Total number of reflections along its length=Length of the wiredistance covered in one reflection along the length

=2×106203=1000003=57735.02≈57000

subject: Physics
q: A double convex lens has power <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></math> and same radii of curvature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></math> of both the surfaces. The radius of curvature of a surface of a plano-convex lens made of the same material with power <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></math> is :
question: A double convex lens has power <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></math> and same radii of curvature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">r</mi></math> of both the surfaces. The radius of curvature of a surface of a plano-convex lens made of the same material with power <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi></math> is :
solution:
1P=(n-1)1R-1-R

1P=(n-1)2R             .........1

11.5P=(n-1)1R-1∞      .......ii

23P=n-1R 

From i and ii

32=R'2R

⇒ R'=R3

subject: Physics
q: Light is incident from a medium into air at two possible angles of incidence (A) $20^{\circ}$ and (B) $40^{\circ}$. In the medium light travels $3.0 \mathrm{~cm}$ in $0.2 \mathrm{~ns}$. The ray will :
question: Light is incident from a medium into air at two possible angles of incidence (A) $20^{\circ}$ and (B) $40^{\circ}$. In the medium light travels $3.0 \mathrm{~cm}$ in $0.2 \mathrm{~ns}$. The ray will :
options: suffer total internal reflection in both cases (A) and (B)
suffer total internal reflection in case (B) only
have partial reflection and partial transmission in case (B)
solution:
Velocity of light in medium
$$
\mathrm{V}_{\text {med }}=\frac{3 \mathrm{~cm}}{0.2 \mathrm{~ns}}=\frac{3 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}}{0.2 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~s}}=1.5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}
$$
Refractive index of the medium
$$
\mu=\frac{V_{\text {air }}}{V_{\text {med }}}=\frac{3 \times 10^8}{1.5}=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}
$$
As $\mu=\frac{1}{\sin C}$
$$
\therefore \sin \mathrm{C}=\frac{1}{\mu}=\frac{1}{2}=30^{\circ}
$$
Condition of TIR is angle of incidence i must be greater than critical angle. Hence ray will suffer TIR in case of $(B)\left(i=40^{\circ}>30^{\circ}\right)$ only.

subject: Physics
q: For an object placed at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> from a lens, a sharp focused image is observed on a screen placed at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from the lens. A glass plate of refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> and thickness <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is introduced between lens and screen such that the glass plate plane faces parallel to the screen. By what distance should the object be shifted so that a sharp focused image is observed again on the screen?
question: For an object placed at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> from a lens, a sharp focused image is observed on a screen placed at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from the lens. A glass plate of refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn></math> and thickness <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is introduced between lens and screen such that the glass plate plane faces parallel to the screen. By what distance should the object be shifted so that a sharp focused image is observed again on the screen?
solution:
Given:
object using the lens formula:
\(\begin{aligned}& \frac{1}{f}=\frac{1}{v \prime}-\frac{1}{w^{\prime}} \\\Rightarrow & \frac{21}{240} \mathrm{~cm}=\frac{3}{35} \mathrm{~cm}-\frac{1}{u^{\prime}} \\\Rightarrow \frac{1}{u^{\prime}} & =\frac{3}{35} \mathrm{~cm}-\frac{21}{240} \mathrm{~cm} \\\Rightarrow & \frac{1}{u^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{5}\left[\frac{3}{7} \mathrm{~cm}-\frac{21}{48} \mathrm{~cm}\right] \\\Rightarrow \frac{1}{u^{\prime}} & =\frac{1}{5}\left[\frac{144-147}{336}\right] \\\Rightarrow u^{\prime} & =-560 \mathrm{~cm}=-5.6 \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}\)
Hence, the object should be at \(5.6 \mathrm{~m}\) in front of the lens.
Hence, the oblect should be moved by 5.6-24= \(3.2 \mathrm{~cm}\).

subject: Physics
q: A microscope is focused on an object at the bottom of a bucket. If liquid with refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>5</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math> is poured inside the bucket, then microscope have to be raised by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mi>cm</mi></math> to focus the object again. The height of the liquid in the bucket is :
question: A microscope is focused on an object at the bottom of a bucket. If liquid with refractive index <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>5</mn><mn>3</mn></mfrac></math> is poured inside the bucket, then microscope have to be raised by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn><mi>cm</mi></math> to focus the object again. The height of the liquid in the bucket is :
solution:
In this case shift is given by, shift=d1-1μ=30 cm

⇒d1-153=30

⇒d=30×52=75 cm
